full_response:
Let  

• frame 𝐀  ≡ rest frame of USS Argonaut,  
• frame 𝐁 ≡ rest frame of USS Barracuda,  
• the common x–axis lie along the submarines’ longitudinal directions,  
• the positive x–direction point from the Argonaut toward the Barracuda,  
• the origins of both frames be chosen so that they coincide with the event  
  “Argonaut-head meets Barracuda-tail (torpedo is fired)”.

Put  
β = v/c , γ = 1/√(1–β²) , L₀ = proper length of each submarine.

-------------------------------------------------------------
Frame 𝐀 (Argonaut at rest)

At the instant the torpedo is fired we set t_A = 0.  
Argonaut head is fixed at x_A = 0, so the firing event has

         E : (ct_A , x_A , y_A , z_A) = (0 , 0 , 0 , 0).

For reference, at this same Argonaut time (t_A = 0)

 Argonaut tail             : x_A = –L₀  
 Barracuda tail (coincident with head of Argonaut) : x_A = 0  
 Barracuda head            : x_A = +L₀/γ      (length–contracted).

-------------------------------------------------------------
Frame 𝐁 (Barracuda at rest)

Frame B moves with velocity –v (i.e. β<0) with respect to frame A.  
Using the Lorentz transformation  

  t_B = γ( t_A + (v/c²) x_A ),  
  x_B = γ( x_A + v t_A ),

and inserting t_A = 0 , x_A = 0 for event E gives

         E : (ct_B , x_B , y_B , z_B) = (0 , 0 , 0 , 0).

At the same Barracuda time t_B = 0 one finds

 Barracuda head      : x_B = +L₀  
 Argonaut tail       : x_B = –L₀/γ    (length–contracted)  
 Argonaut head (event E) : x_B = 0.

-------------------------------------------------------------
Thus the space-time coordinates of the torpedo-firing event are

 Frame A : (ct_A , x_A , y_A , z_A) = (0 , 0 , 0 , 0)  
 Frame B : (ct_B , x_B , y_B , z_B) = (0 , 0 , 0 , 0).

\boxed{\text{Firing event: } E_A = (0,0,0,0),\; E_B = (0,0,0,0)}  

(The additional positions of each submarine’s ends at that instant are given above for completeness.)

Confidence: 48%
